question: A Node.js worker uses `zbc.createWorker({ taskType, taskHandler })` with `axios.get(...).then(...)` inside the handler. A teammate proposes rewriting it with `const r = await axios.get(...)` to "block on the response". What does the docs say will happen?

options.text: Even the seemingly blocking `await` form is still executed in a non-blocking fashion — JavaScript cannot truly block — so the worker scales the same way.
The `await` form will block Node.js's only thread and serialize jobs to one at a time.
The Node.js SDK refuses to register a handler that uses `async/await`; the test will throw at startup.
It only works if you also configure a thread pool, because Node.js requires one for concurrent job activation.

a.text: The docs make this explicit: reactive programming is so deep in JavaScript that it is impossible to write truly blocking code, and code that looks blocking is still executed non-blockingly. Node.js workers scale well via the event loop.

b.text: `await` does not block the Node.js event loop; it suspends the async function and lets the loop service other callbacks. The docs note JavaScript cannot really block.

c.text: The SDK accepts ordinary `async` handlers; there is no such restriction described in the docs.

d.text: Node.js needs no specific worker thread pool — the Camunda 8 Node.js client itself uses reactive programming internally and relies on the event loop.

explanation: The Node.js section is unequivocal: JavaScript cannot really block, so even a `await axios.get(...)` style handler is still executed by the event loop and the worker scales without any thread-pool tuning. The Camunda 8 Node.js client itself is reactive internally.
